Add scene context metadata (POV, location, timeframe) with filterable navigator
description:
## Problem
Writers lose narrative orientation when drafting across many scenes because key context (POV, location, timeframe) is not consistently visible or searchable. This increases continuity mistakes and slows revision.

## Target user
Multi-chapter fiction writers who juggle multiple POV characters, locations, and timelines while drafting and revising.

## Outcome / KPI
- Reduce continuity-related scene revisions per manuscript
- Increase weekly active use of scene navigator filters
- Decrease average time to find target scene during revision

## Scope
- Add scene-level metadata fields: `POV character`, `location`, `timeframe` (free text v1 with optional suggestions from existing worldbuilding entities)
- Surface metadata in scene cards/navigator rows and at top of scene editor
- Add filter controls in chapter/scene navigator for POV, location, and timeframe
- Keep metadata editable from both scene settings and scene card quick-edit
- Persist metadata for use in existing timeline/continuity workflows

## Non-goals
- Automatic contradiction detection rules
- Complex ontology management for tags/entities
- AI-generated scene metadata

## Acceptance criteria
- User can set/edit POV, location, and timeframe on any scene
- Scene navigator can filter by each metadata field independently and in combination
- Active filters are clearly visible and can be reset in one action
- Metadata appears in the scene editor header without obstructing writing flow
- Metadata values persist across reload and are available in timeline views

## Priority rationale
This builds a practical writing-flow advantage aligned with Living Writer/Campfire inspiration: keep structure context visible while drafting, not hidden in separate planning screens. It also compounds value of in-flight navigator and timeline features already on the roadmap.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

<plan>

## Goal
Add scene-level context metadata (POV character, location, timeframe) with visible in-editor display and filterable corkboard navigation.

## Scope and files

**Modified files (6):**
1. `src/types/scene.ts` - Add optional metadata fields: `pov?`, `location?`, `timeframe?`
2. `src/lib/scenes.ts` - Add `updateSceneMetadata` helper function
3. `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` - Add metadata row to editor, wire up metadata updates
4. `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css` - Metadata row styles
5. `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx` - Add filter state and filtering logic
6. `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx` - Add metadata display line

**New files (4):**
1. `src/components/MetadataRow/MetadataRow.tsx` - Metadata input fields for editor
2. `src/components/MetadataRow/MetadataRow.module.css` - Metadata row styles
3. `src/components/MetadataFilters/MetadataFilters.tsx` - Filter controls for corkboard
4. `src/components/MetadataFilters/MetadataFilters.module.css` - Filter styles

## Step-by-step plan

### Phase 1: Data model extension

**Step 1.1: Add metadata fields to Scene type**
- File: `src/types/scene.ts`
- Add optional fields: `pov?: string`, `location?: string`, `timeframe?: string`
- All optional to support existing scenes without metadata

**Step 1.2: Add metadata update helper**
- File: `src/lib/scenes.ts`
- Add function: `updateSceneMetadata(storyId: string, sceneId: string, metadata: { pov?: string; location?: string; timeframe?: string }): void`
- Fetches scene, updates metadata fields, persists to localStorage
- Preserves existing fields (title, summary, status, order)

### Phase 2: Editor metadata row

**Step 2.1: Build MetadataRow component**
- Files: `src/components/MetadataRow/MetadataRow.tsx`, `.module.css`
- Props: `pov?: string`, `location?: string`, `timeframe?: string`, `onChange: (field: string, value: string) => void`, `readOnly?: boolean`
- Render: Three inline input groups with labels "POV", "Location", "Timeframe"
- Inputs: Text inputs with placeholder "Add POV" when empty
- Keyboard: Tab order left-to-right, all inputs accessible
- Style: Compact horizontal row, labels always visible (`--font-size-sm`, `--color-elephant-500`), inputs use existing form input styles
- Accessibility: Explicit labels, not placeholder-only

**Step 2.2: Integrate metadata row into editor**
- File: `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`
- In `editorHeader`, add `<MetadataRow>` below title input, above editor canvas
- Pass current scene metadata as props
- Wire `onChange` to call `updateSceneMetadata` with debouncing (500ms, same as content auto-save)
- Handle null/undefined selectedScene gracefully (hide metadata row if no scene selected)

**Step 2.3: Style metadata row in editor layout**
- File: `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css`
- Add `.metadataRow` class: horizontal layout, spacing between fields
- Use existing token values for padding, borders, colors
- Maintain visual rhythm with existing editor header

### Phase 3: Corkboard filtering

**Step 3.1: Build MetadataFilters component**
- Files: `src/components/MetadataFilters/MetadataFilters.tsx`, `.module.css`
- Props: `povOptions: string[]`, `locationOptions: string[]`, `timeframeOptions: string[]`, `activePov: string | null`, `activeLocation: string | null`, `activeTimeframe: string | null`, `onFilterChange: (field: string, value: string | null) => void`, `onClearAll: () => void`
- Render: Three filter dropdowns/selects labeled "POV", "Location", "Timeframe", plus "Clear filters" button
- Dropdowns: "All" default option, then sorted unique values from scenes
- Clear all button: Visible when any filter active, resets all to null
- Style: Horizontal row, compact controls, grayscale tokens only
- Accessibility: Labels, keyboard-navigable dropdowns

**Step 3.2: Add filter state to Corkboard**
- File: `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx`
- Add state: `const [activePov, setActivePov] = useState<string | null>(null)` (same for location, timeframe)
- Derive unique metadata values from scenes array: `const povOptions = Array.from(new Set(scenes.map(s => s.pov).filter(Boolean)))`
- Filter scenes array before rendering: scenes that match all active filters (AND logic)
- Display filtered count: "Showing X of Y scenes" when filters active
- Empty state: "No scenes match filters" with "Clear filters" action

**Step 3.3: Integrate filters into corkboard toolbar**
- File: `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx`
- In toolbar (currently has `<ViewModeSwitch>`), add `<MetadataFilters>` in `toolbarRight` area per Design brief
- Pass filter state and callbacks
- Ensure toolbar remains single row on desktop, wraps gracefully on tablet/mobile

### Phase 4: Metadata display in scene cards

**Step 4.1: Add metadata line to SceneCard**
- Files: `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx`, `.module.css`
- Between `summary` and `footer`, add new `.metadata` section
- Render: Three compact chips/labels: "POV: {value}", "Loc: {location}", "Time: {timeframe}"
- Hide chip if value is empty/undefined
- Truncate long values with ellipsis, show full value in title attribute (tooltip)
- Style: Small font (`--font-size-xs`), muted color (`--color-cement-400`), horizontal layout with spacing

**Step 4.2: Add quick-edit affordance (optional for v1)**
- File: `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx`
- Add "Edit context" link/button in footer (next to status select)
- On click: Open inline popover or inline edit mode with three text inputs
- On save: Call `onMetadataChange` callback (added to props), which calls `updateSceneMetadata`
- Accessibility: Keyboard-accessible, focus management
- Note: If time-constrained, defer quick-edit to v2; users can edit in editor mode first

### Phase 5: Empty states and polish

**Step 5.1: Handle missing metadata gracefully**
- MetadataRow: Show placeholders "Add POV" when empty, not blank inputs
- SceneCard: Omit metadata chips entirely if all three fields empty (not "POV: —")
- Corkboard: If no scenes have metadata yet, hide filter controls or show muted empty state

**Step 5.2: Filter persistence (optional)**
- Store active filters in sessionStorage with key `story-${storyId}-filters`
- Restore on corkboard mount
- Clear on unmount or when switching stories
- Note: sessionStorage, not localStorage, to avoid sticky filters across sessions

**Step 5.3: Success feedback for metadata saves**
- Optional: Show subtle "Saved" indicator near metadata row for 1-2 seconds after save
- Keep low-friction, non-blocking (not toast/alert)
- Use existing auto-save pattern from editor if implemented

### Phase 6: Responsive behavior

**Step 6.1: Metadata row responsive layout**
- File: `src/components/MetadataRow/MetadataRow.module.css`
- Desktop: Three fields in one horizontal row, equal width
- Tablet (768-1023px): Three fields may reduce width but stay horizontal
- Mobile (< 768px): Stack vertically, full-width inputs

**Step 6.2: Filter controls responsive layout**
- File: `src/components/MetadataFilters/MetadataFilters.module.css`
- Desktop: Horizontal row in toolbar right area
- Tablet: May wrap to second row, stays above grid
- Mobile: Compact horizontal row with smaller dropdowns, or collapse to single "Filter" button with modal/drawer

## Test plan

**Unit tests (if framework exists):**
- `src/lib/scenes.ts`: Test `updateSceneMetadata` updates specific fields without affecting others
- Metadata extraction: Test deriving unique POV/location/timeframe values from scene arrays
- Filter logic: Test AND combination (scene with POV=A, Location=B passes filter {POV: A, Location: B})

**Integration tests:**
1. Add metadata to scene in editor → Verify persists to localStorage
2. Reload page → Verify metadata displays in editor and scene card
3. Set POV filter in corkboard → Verify only scenes with that POV visible
4. Set multiple filters (POV + Location) → Verify only scenes matching both criteria visible
5. Clear filters → Verify all scenes return
6. Create new scene → Verify starts with empty metadata (no crash)

**Manual testing:**

- **Keyboard accessibility:**
  - Tab through metadata inputs in logical order (POV → Location → Timeframe)
  - Tab through filter dropdowns
  - Keyboard-accessible "Clear filters" button

- **Visual clarity:**
  - Labels always visible (not just placeholders)
  - Metadata chips in cards truncate long values without breaking layout
  - Filter controls clearly show active state

- **Responsive:**
  - Desktop: Metadata row horizontal, filter controls in toolbar right
  - Tablet: Metadata row stays horizontal, filters may wrap
  - Mobile: Metadata row stacks vertically, filter controls compact or drawer

- **Data integrity:**
  - Scenes without metadata don't crash or show "undefined"
  - Updating metadata preserves title, summary, status, order
  - Filter state doesn't persist inappropriately across sessions

- **Edge cases:**
  - Very long POV/location/timeframe values (50+ chars): Truncate with ellipsis, tooltip shows full value
  - Special characters in metadata (quotes, emojis): Display correctly, no encoding issues
  - All scenes lack metadata: Filter controls hidden or show empty state
  - No scenes match active filters: Show clear empty state with "Clear filters" action

**Commands:**
```bash
npm run dev
# Visit http://localhost:3000/story/test-1
# Test metadata input in editor
# Switch to corkboard, test filters
# Check localStorage: story-test-1-scenes should include metadata fields
```

## Risks and mitigations

**Risk 1: Metadata fields too open-ended**
- Problem: Users enter inconsistent values ("POV: Alice" vs "Alice" vs "alice")
- Impact: Filters less useful, many options in dropdown
- Mitigation v1: Accept free text, show sorted unique values. Future: Add autocomplete from existing values or worldbuilding entities.

**Risk 2: Filter UX complexity**
- Problem: AND logic with three filters may be confusing (empty results when too restrictive)
- Impact: Users frustrated by "No scenes" message
- Mitigation: Show filtered count "X of Y scenes", make "Clear filters" prominent. Future: Add OR toggle or filter by "any of" logic.

**Risk 3: Performance with many unique metadata values**
- Problem: 50+ unique POV characters = large dropdown, slow rendering
- Impact: Laggy filter controls
- Mitigation: Limit dropdown to most recent 20 values, add search input if needed. Use memoization for unique value extraction.

**Risk 4: Metadata row clutters editor**
- Problem: Three input fields take vertical space, reduce canvas area
- Impact: Writers feel cramped
- Mitigation: Keep row compact (single line), use small font, consider collapsible metadata section in future if feedback is negative.

**Risk 5: Quick-edit in scene card adds complexity**
- Problem: Inline edit mode in card is non-trivial UI work
- Impact: Schedule risk for v1 delivery
- Mitigation: Defer quick-edit to v2; users can edit metadata in editor mode for v1. Card displays read-only metadata.

## Rollback strategy

**If critical bugs found:**
1. Revert PR merge commit
2. Existing scenes without metadata continue working (optional fields)
3. No data loss: Metadata fields added, never removed from existing scenes

**Graceful degradation:**
- If metadata undefined: Treat as empty string, show placeholder
- If filter logic fails: Show all scenes unfiltered (fail open, not fail closed)
- If MetadataRow crashes: Wrap in error boundary, show "Metadata unavailable" message

## Observability

**Logs:**
- `src/lib/scenes.ts`: Console log on `updateSceneMetadata` → "Updated metadata for scene {id}: {pov}, {location}, {timeframe}"
- Corkboard: Log filter changes → "Applied filters: POV={value}, Location={value}, Timeframe={value}"
- Log filtered scene count → "Showing {filtered} of {total} scenes"

**Metrics (future):**
- Track metadata field completion rate (% of scenes with POV, location, timeframe)
- Track filter usage frequency (how often users apply filters)
- Track average unique values per metadata field (helps inform autocomplete UX)

**Traces:**
- Not applicable for localStorage v1

## Acceptance mapping

| Acceptance Criteria | Implementation |
|---------------------|----------------|
| User can set/edit POV, location, and timeframe on any scene | MetadataRow in editor with three text inputs → updateSceneMetadata saves to localStorage |
| Scene navigator can filter by each metadata field independently and in combination | MetadataFilters in corkboard toolbar with three dropdowns → filter logic (AND) → filtered scenes array |
| Active filters are clearly visible and can be reset in one action | Filter dropdowns show selected values, "Clear filters" button visible when filters active → resets all to null |
| Metadata appears in the scene editor header without obstructing writing flow | MetadataRow placed below title, above canvas, compact horizontal layout, grayscale tokens |
| Metadata values persist across reload and are available in timeline views | localStorage persistence via updateSceneMetadata → getScenes loads metadata → scene cards display metadata |

## Handoff

**Implementation readiness:**
- ✅ Product requirements reviewed
- ✅ Design brief reviewed
- ✅ Current Scene type and Corkboard/SceneCard architecture understood
- ✅ Implementation plan complete with 6 phases

**Code Monkey: Key implementation notes**
1. **Phase order:** Data model → Editor → Filters → Card display → Polish → Responsive
2. **Optional fields:** All metadata fields are optional, support existing scenes without metadata
3. **Filter logic:** AND combination (scene must match all active filters to be visible)
4. **Quick-edit in cards:** Optional for v1, can defer to v2 if time-constrained
5. **Grayscale tokens only:** Reference `src/styles/tokens.css` for all colors
6. **Refer to Design brief** for exact field order (POV, Location, Timeframe) and visual hierarchy

Assign to: Code Monkey




---

## REVISION — Code Review Fixes (BIN-73)

### Changes requested by Code Reviewer

The following fixes address the Code Reviewer's findings before approval.

---

### Rev-Step 1: Fix `useState` → `useRef` for debounce timer (critical)

**File:** `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`

Replace the debounce timer declaration and all usages:

```ts
// BEFORE (broken — does not persist across re-renders)
const metadataDebounceRef = useState<ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | null>(null);
// ...
if (metadataDebounceRef[0]) clearTimeout(metadataDebounceRef[0]);
metadataDebounceRef[0] = setTimeout(() => { ... }, 500);

// AFTER (correct — ref persists across renders)
const metadataDebounceRef = useRef<ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | null>(null);
// ...
if (metadataDebounceRef.current) clearTimeout(metadataDebounceRef.current);
metadataDebounceRef.current = setTimeout(() => { ... }, 500);
```

Also update the `import` at the top of the file to include `useRef`:

```ts
import { useState, useEffect, useRef, use } from 'react';
```

---

### Rev-Step 2: Add unmount cleanup for debounce timer (nit)

**File:** `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`

Add a `useEffect` cleanup to prevent the stale metadata save firing after the component unmounts (e.g., user navigates away mid-debounce):

```ts
useEffect(() => {
  return () => {
    if (metadataDebounceRef.current) {
      clearTimeout(metadataDebounceRef.current);
    }
  };
}, []);
```

Place this after the other `useEffect` hooks near the top of the component.

---

### Rev-Step 3: Remove `console.log` statements from production code (major)

**File 1:** `src/lib/scenes.ts` — line ~131
Delete the `console.log` statement that fires on every `updateSceneMetadata` call.

**File 2:** `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx` — line ~88
Delete the `console.log` statement that fires on every filter change.

No replacement needed — these are debug/observability statements, not errors. The Observability section in the original plan described these as desirable; they should be omitted from the shipped code. Future observability can be added via a proper logging utility if needed.

---

### Rev-Step 4: Add `idPrefix` prop to `MetadataRow` (minor)

**File:** `src/components/MetadataRow/MetadataRow.tsx`

1. Add optional `idPrefix?: string` prop (default: `'meta'`):

```ts
interface MetadataRowProps {
  pov?: string;
  location?: string;
  timeframe?: string;
  onChange: (field: string, value: string) => void;
  readOnly?: boolean;
  idPrefix?: string;  // NEW — default 'meta'
}
```

2. Use `idPrefix` when constructing `id` and `htmlFor` attributes:

```ts
const prefix = idPrefix ?? 'meta';
// ...
<label htmlFor={`${prefix}-pov`}>POV</label>
<input id={`${prefix}-pov`} ... />

<label htmlFor={`${prefix}-location`}>Location</label>
<input id={`${prefix}-location`} ... />

<label htmlFor={`${prefix}-timeframe`}>Timeframe</label>
<input id={`${prefix}-timeframe`} ... />
```

3. The existing call site in `page.tsx` does not need to pass `idPrefix` (default `'meta'` preserves existing behavior).

---

## Validation for this revision

After applying fixes, verify:

1. **Debounce works correctly:** Type rapidly in POV/Location/Timeframe fields → localStorage should update only once, ~500ms after the last keystroke. Verify with DevTools → Application → Local Storage (only one save event per typing burst).

2. **No console output:** Open DevTools console, perform metadata edits and filter changes → no log output from `updateSceneMetadata` or filter change handlers.

3. **Unmount cleanup:** Navigate away from the story editor mid-debounce (fast keystroke → back button) → no console errors about setState on unmounted component.

4. **MetadataRow with idPrefix:** Check DOM in DevTools → labels have correct `for` attributes matching input `id` attributes.

5. **Build passes:** `npm run build` → no TypeScript errors.

</plan>

title: Add manuscript-wide find and replace with scoped filters and preview
description:
## Problem As manuscripts grow, writers struggle to make consistent edits across scenes (names, terms, style fixes). Current navigation support helps jumping, but there is no dedicated manuscript-wide find/replace workflow with context previews. ## Target user Writers editing medium-to-long manuscripts who need fast global cleanup without manually opening every scene. ## Outcome / KPI - Reduce time to complete global revision tasks (character renames, terminology updates) - Increase completion rate of revision sessions - Increase weekly active editing sessions on manuscripts with 10+ scenes ## Scope - Add manuscript-wide Find panel that searches title/body across all scenes in a story. - Show grouped results by chapter/scene with snippet previews and match counts. - Support filters (current scene, current chapter, whole manuscript) and case/whole-word toggles. - Add Replace and Replace All with preview/confirmation, including per-result skip controls. - Keep keyboard-first workflow (`Cmd/Ctrl+F` in scene, `Shift+Cmd/Ctrl+F` for manuscript-wide search). ## Non-goals - Regex/advanced query syntax in v1 - Cross-story/global library search - AI rewrite suggestions in the search flow ## Acceptance criteria - Writer can run manuscript-wide search and view contextual matches across scenes. - Clicking a result jumps to the exact scene and highlighted occurrence. - Replace supports single replacement and bulk replacement with confirmation. - Replace actions provide undo for the current editing session. - Search/replace remains performant on manuscripts with at least 100 scenes. ## Priority rationale Global editing speed is a high-frequency pain point for serious writers and an area where polished tools stand out. Shipping this now complements existing structure/navigation work and improves real revision throughput. ## Handoff Assign to: Market Research ## Goal Add a manuscript-wide find and replace panel to the story editor. Writers can search across all scene titles and summaries, view grouped contextual results, and replace individual or all matches — with session undo — using keyboard-first shortcuts. ## Scope and files **New files:** - `src/components/ManuscriptFindPanel/ManuscriptFindPanel.tsx` - `src/components/ManuscriptFindPanel/ManuscriptFindPanel.module.css` - `src/lib/findReplace.ts` **Modified files:** - `src/lib/scenes.ts` — add `updateSceneFields()` helper - `src/types/scene.ts` — no changes needed; `title` and `summary` are the searchable fields - `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` — add panel state, keyboard shortcuts, render panel - `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css` — layout adjustments when panel is open ## Step-by-step plan ### Step 1 — Add `updateSceneFields` to `src/lib/scenes.ts` Add at the bottom of `scenes.ts`: ```ts export function updateSceneFields( storyId: string, sceneId: string, updates: Partial> ): void { if (typeof window === 'undefined') return; const scenes = getScenes(storyId); const scene = scenes.find((s) => s.id === sceneId); if (!scene) return; Object.assign(scene, updates); const storageKey = `story-${storyId}-scenes`; localStorage.setItem(storageKey, JSON.stringify(scenes)); } ``` ### Step 2 — Create `src/lib/findReplace.ts` Define types and pure search logic (no side effects): ```ts export type SearchField = 'title' | 'summary'; export type SearchScope = 'manuscript' | 'chapter' | 'scene'; export interface SearchOptions { query: string; scope: SearchScope; caseSensitive: boolean; wholeWord: boolean; } export interface Match { sceneId: string; chapter: string; field: SearchField; offset: number; // character index in field value length: number; // match length snippet: string; // ~80 chars of context around match } export interface SceneResult { sceneId: string; sceneTitle: string; chapter: string; matches: Match[]; } ``` Implement `buildRegex(query, options): RegExp | null`: - Return null if query is empty - Escape regex special chars - Apply `\b` word boundary for wholeWord - Apply `g` + optionally `i` flags Implement `findMatches(scenes, options, currentSceneId?, currentChapter?): SceneResult[]`: - Filter scenes by scope (manuscript = all, chapter = scenes in currentChapter, scene = currentSceneId only) - For each scene, run regex against `title` and `summary` - Build `Match` objects with `offset`, `length`, and a 80-char snippet (40 chars before + match + 40 chars after) - Group by scene into `SceneResult[]`, skip scenes with 0 matches - Return results in scene order Implement `applyReplace(value: string, match: Match, replaceText: string): string`: - Returns the field value with the single match replaced (using offset + length) Implement `applyReplaceAll(scenes, matches, replaceText): { sceneId, field, newValue }[]`: - For each sceneId+field combination, apply all matches in reverse order (highest offset first) to avoid offset drift - Return array of `{ sceneId, field, newValue }` for caller to persist ### Step 3 — Create `ManuscriptFindPanel.tsx` **Props interface:** ```ts interface ManuscriptFindPanelProps { storyId: string; scenes: Scene[]; currentSceneId: string | null; currentChapter: string | null; isOpen: boolean; initialScope: SearchScope; onClose: () => void; onSceneJump: (sceneId: string) => void; onScenesChanged: () => void; // triggers parent to refresh scenes } ``` **Component state:** - `query: string` — find input - `replaceText: string` — replace input - `scope: SearchScope` — current scope toggle - `caseSensitive: boolean` — toggle, default false - `wholeWord: boolean` — toggle, default false - `results: SceneResult[]` — current search results - `undoStack: UndoEntry[]` — session undo **UndoEntry type:** ```ts interface UndoEntry { storyId: string; changes: Array<{ sceneId: string; field: SearchField; oldValue: string; newValue: string }>; } ``` **Behaviour:** - On mount, focus the find input - Debounce search 300ms after query/scope/caseSensitive/wholeWord changes using `useEffect` + `setTimeout`/`clearTimeout` - Show result count header: "N matches across M scenes" - Group results: chapter heading → scene subheading → match rows with snippet - Highlight the matched text in the snippet using `` or `` with CSS - Per-match row: snippet text + "Replace" button + "Skip" (no-op visual affordance in v1) - Below inputs: "Replace All" button (disabled if no results) - Undo: "Undo (N)" button shown when undoStack.length > 0 - Escape key closes panel **Single Replace flow:** 1. Capture old field value from scene 2. Call `applyReplace(oldValue, match, replaceText)` → newValue 3. Call `updateSceneFields(storyId, sceneId, { [field]: newValue })` 4. Push `{ storyId, changes: [{ sceneId, field, oldValue, newValue }] }` to undoStack 5. Call `onScenesChanged()` to refresh parent scene list 6. Re-run search to refresh results **Replace All flow:** 1. Show inline confirmation: "Replace all N matches? [Confirm] [Cancel]" 2. On confirm: call `applyReplaceAll(scenes, allMatches, replaceText)` 3. For each change, call `updateSceneFields` 4. Push batch `UndoEntry` with all changes to undoStack 5. Call `onScenesChanged()` 6. Re-run search (should return 0 results) 7. Show success banner: "Replaced N occurrences · Undo" **Undo flow:** 1. Pop top entry from undoStack 2. For each change in entry, call `updateSceneFields(storyId, sceneId, { [field]: oldValue })` 3. Call `onScenesChanged()` 4. Re-run search **Keyboard behaviour:** - `Tab` cycles through: find input → replace input → scope toggles → case toggle → whole word toggle → results - `Enter` in find input: move focus to first result - `Escape`: close panel (call `onClose`) ### Step 4 — Create `ManuscriptFindPanel.module.css` Follow design brief layout and token spec: **Desktop (default):** Panel fixed right within editor sidebar area, 320px wide. Key CSS classes: - `.panel` — `display: flex; flex-direction: column; height: 100%; background: var(--color-white); border-left: 1px solid var(--color-fashion-300)` - `.panelHeader` — title "Find & Replace" + close button, padding `var(--spacing-md)` - `.inputs` — stacked find/replace inputs with shared border style - `.input` — `width: 100%; padding: var(--spacing-sm) var(--spacing-md); border: 1px solid var(--color-fashion-300); color: var(--color-black-700); background: var(--color-white)` - `.toggles` — row of scope pills + case/word toggles - `.pill` / `.pillActive` — scope toggles (`var(--color-polar-100)` / `var(--color-black-700)` bg with `var(--color-white)` text when active) - `.iconToggle` / `.iconToggleActive` — case/word toggles (small buttons) - `.resultsArea` — `flex: 1; overflow-y: auto` - `.chapterGroup` — chapter heading in `--color-cement-400` font-size small uppercase - `.sceneGroup` — scene heading in `--color-elephant-500` font-weight 500 - `.matchRow` — result row, `padding: var(--spacing-sm) var(--spacing-md)`, hover: `var(--color-fog-200)` bg - `.snippet` — match text with `` styled: `background: var(--color-fog-200); color: var(--color-black-700)` - `.replaceBtn` — small inline button - `.actions` — bottom bar with Replace All + Undo - `.undoBtn` — visible only when undoStack > 0 - `.confirmBanner` — inline confirmation for Replace All - `.successBanner` — success state with replaced count + Undo link - `.emptyState` — "No matches" message in `--color-cement-400` **Responsive:** - `@media (max-width: 768px)`: slide-over panel with `position: fixed; right: 0; top: 0; height: 100vh; z-index: 200; box-shadow: ...` - `@media (max-width: 480px)`: full-height overlay: `width: 100vw` ### Step 5 — Update `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` 1. Add state at top of component: ```ts const [findPanelOpen, setFindPanelOpen] = useState(false); const [findInitialScope, setFindInitialScope] = useState('manuscript'); const [currentSceneId] = useState(null); // TODO: set from editor selection const [currentChapter] = useState(null); // TODO: set from editor selection ``` 2. Add keyboard shortcut handler in `useEffect`: ```ts useEffect(() => { const handleKeyDown = (e: KeyboardEvent) => { const mod = e.metaKey || e.ctrlKey; if (mod && e.key === 'f') { e.preventDefault(); setFindInitialScope('scene'); setFindPanelOpen(true); } if (mod && e.shiftKey && e.key === 'F') { e.preventDefault(); setFindInitialScope('manuscript'); setFindPanelOpen(true); } }; document.addEventListener('keydown', handleKeyDown); return () => document.removeEventListener('keydown', handleKeyDown); }, []); ``` 3. In the editor layout, replace the sidebar's "Notes" placeholder section with: ```tsx
{findPanelOpen ? ( setFindPanelOpen(false)} onSceneJump={handleSceneClick} onScenesChanged={refreshScenes} /> ) : (

Notes

Notes and outline will appear here

Press ⌘F for find · ⇧⌘F for manuscript search

)}
``` 4. Import `ManuscriptFindPanel` and `SearchScope` at top of file. ### Step 6 — Update `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css` Add `.placeholderHint` style: ```css .placeholderHint { font-size: 0.75rem; color: var(--color-cement-400); margin-top: var(--spacing-sm); } ``` No layout changes needed — the panel replaces the Notes section within the existing sidebar container. ## Test plan **Unit / logic tests (manual in browser):** 1. Open a story → confirm `Notes` placeholder shows with keyboard hint 2. Press `Cmd/Ctrl+F` → panel opens with scope set to "Scene" 3. Press `Shift+Cmd/Ctrl+F` → panel opens with scope set to "Manuscript" 4. Type a query that exists in a scene summary → results appear within 300ms debounce 5. Type a query with no matches → "No matches" empty state shown 6. Toggle case-sensitive → results update correctly 7. Toggle whole word → partial-word matches excluded 8. Click "Replace" on one match → scene updated, undo stack +1, "Undo (1)" visible 9. Click "Undo (1)" → change reverted, results re-run, undo stack empty 10. Click "Replace All" → confirmation shows with exact count 11. Confirm → all matches replaced, "Replaced N occurrences · Undo" success banner 12. Click Undo in banner → all batch changes reverted 13. Press Escape → panel closes 14. Click result row → `onSceneJump` called (console.log confirms) 15. Switch scope: manuscript / chapter / scene → results filtered correctly 16. Test with 10+ mock scenes by adding them to localStorage manually **Responsive tests:** - Resize to 768px → panel becomes slide-over - Resize to 480px → panel becomes full-width overlay **Accessibility tests:** - Tab through all controls sequentially - Screen reader: confirm inputs have labels, results have aria-live region - Visible focus ring on all interactive elements **Performance test:** - Manually add 100 scenes to localStorage, run search → results render without lag ## Risks and mitigations | Risk | Mitigation | |------|------------| | Regex offset drift when doing multiple replaces in same field | `applyReplaceAll` processes matches in reverse order (highest offset first) | | Replace All confirmation skipped by fast clicking | "Replace All" button disabled during confirmation state; confirm button requires explicit click | | Undo stack grows unbounded | Cap at 50 entries; oldest entries dropped silently (session only, low risk) | | Empty query triggers full search | `findMatches` returns `[]` if query is empty; no regex built | | `summary` field used as "body" is short (~2-3 sentences) | This is the only searchable text in v1; matches the existing data model; noted for future when per-scene editor body is added | ## Rollback strategy All changes are additive: new component files + a new lib function + new state in `page.tsx`. Rollback = delete new files and revert the `page.tsx` edits. No schema migrations or localStorage format changes required. ## Observability - `console.log` on each Replace and Replace All action with sceneId and field (matches existing scene update logging pattern) - No new metrics in v1; usage visible via console during QA ## Acceptance mapping | Acceptance criterion | Implementation | |----------------------|----------------| | Writer can run manuscript-wide search and view contextual matches across scenes | `findMatches` across all scenes, `ManuscriptFindPanel` renders grouped results | | Clicking a result jumps to the exact scene and highlighted occurrence | `onSceneJump(sceneId)` called on row click; highlight via `` in snippet | | Replace supports single replacement and bulk replacement with confirmation | "Replace" button per row; "Replace All" with inline confirmation modal | | Replace actions provide undo for the current editing session | `undoStack` in component state; Undo button restores old field values | | Search/replace remains performant on manuscripts with at least 100 scenes | Debounced 300ms; pure regex over small text fields; no virtual scroll needed for ≤100 | ## Handoff Assign to: Code Monkey

title: Launch Series Bible workspace with shared canon across multiple stories
description:
## Problem
Writers creating trilogies or connected universes cannot manage shared canon across stories in one place. This leads to continuity mistakes, duplicate worldbuilding data, and high manual overhead when reusing characters, locations, and rules.

## Target user
Series fiction writers (2+ manuscripts in one universe) who need a single source of truth for canon while drafting individual books.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase percentage of active users who create a second manuscript in the app
- Reduce continuity-related revisions reported during later draft stages
- Increase reuse rate of existing worldbuilding entities across stories

## Scope
- Add a `Series` container that can include multiple stories
- Create a Series Bible view with shared entities (characters, locations, lore/rules)
- Allow linking shared entities into scenes from any story in the series
- Show cross-story references on each shared entity (where it appears)
- Add guardrail warning when an entity is edited in ways that may affect linked stories

## Non-goals
- Real-time multi-author editing workflows
- Public reader-facing wiki pages
- Full rights/IP management system

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can create a series and add at least two stories to it
- Writer can create shared canon entities once and link them from scenes in multiple stories
- Entity detail shows cross-story usage references with deep links
- Editing a shared entity surfaces a clear impact warning when linked scenes exist
- Existing single-story workflows remain functional for users not using series

## Priority rationale
This builds on the Campfire-inspired worldbuilding direction while creating a differentiation path for long-form series authors, a high-retention segment that needs continuity across books to stay in one tool.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Ship full-fidelity project backup export/import (Story Bundle)
description:
## Problem
Writers fear tool lock-in and data loss when their work includes more than manuscript text (structure, worldbuilding links, revision metadata). Current document export formats are necessary but insufficient for complete project portability and disaster recovery.

## Target user
Serious fiction writers who invest heavily in story structure and metadata and need confidence they can move or restore complete projects at any time.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase trust and retention among users with long-running manuscripts
- Decrease support requests about migration and recovery
- Increase completion rate of backup actions per active story

## Scope
- Add `Export Story Bundle` action that packages full story data (manuscript, chapters/scenes, structure metadata, worldbuilding links, revision data)
- Add `Import Story Bundle` flow to restore a full project into the library
- Include bundle schema versioning and compatibility checks on import
- Add checksum/integrity validation with actionable error states
- Add confirmation summary before import (story name, last updated, included assets)

## Non-goals
- Replacing DOCX/PDF manuscript export workflows
- Multi-user merge conflict resolution for bundle imports
- Cross-app direct importers (e.g., Scrivener-native format) in v1

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can export a story bundle and re-import it into a clean local library with no missing content
- Imported project preserves chapter/scene order and linked metadata relationships
- Import rejects incompatible or corrupted bundles with clear messaging
- Bundle format includes explicit version metadata for forward compatibility handling
- Export/import actions are available without requiring cloud sync opt-in

## Priority rationale
Portability and recovery trust are table stakes for writer adoption and directly support the goal to ship a credible end-to-end writing app. This feature complements manuscript export by preserving full project fidelity.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Launch first-story onboarding flow with guided chapter and scene scaffold
description:
## Problem
New users face a blank-page experience and often fail to reach a meaningful first writing milestone. Without guided setup, activation and early retention suffer.

## Target user
First-time users evaluating the app in their first session, especially writers migrating from generic docs tools.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase activation rate: % of new users who create first story and first scene in one session
- Increase day-1 to day-7 retention for users who complete onboarding
- Reduce first-session drop-off before first 200 words

## Scope
- Add an optional onboarding flow after first story creation with 3 guided steps:
  1. Name story + genre intent
  2. Create first chapter and first scene
  3. Set a lightweight first-session objective (draft/revise/brainstorm)
- Pre-fill the editor with a minimal scaffold (chapter title + scene prompt placeholder)
- Provide skip and dismiss controls so experienced users can bypass onboarding
- Track onboarding completion state per user to avoid repeated prompts

## Non-goals
- Full interactive tutorial for every feature
- Mandatory onboarding gates that block editor access
- Personalized AI coaching in onboarding v1

## Acceptance criteria
- New user is offered onboarding once after first story creation
- User can complete onboarding in under 2 minutes and land in editable first scene
- User can skip onboarding at any step and still access core editor
- Onboarding completion/skip state persists and prevents repeated prompting
- Event instrumentation exists for each onboarding step and completion outcome

## Priority rationale
The app already has strong drafting/planning surface area; onboarding is the missing bridge from sign-up intent to actual writing behavior. Shipping this now should improve activation before adding more advanced features.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

<plan>

## Goal
Launch a 3-step first-story onboarding flow that guides new users from story creation to their first editable scene in under 2 minutes.

## Scope and files

**New files (9):**
1. `src/components/OnboardingModal/OnboardingModal.tsx` - Main modal container with stepper
2. `src/components/OnboardingModal/OnboardingModal.module.css` - Modal styles
3. `src/components/OnboardingModal/StoryNameStep.tsx` - Step 1: name + genre
4. `src/components/OnboardingModal/ChapterSceneStep.tsx` - Step 2: chapter/scene scaffold
5. `src/components/OnboardingModal/ObjectiveStep.tsx` - Step 3: session objective
6. `src/components/OnboardingModal/ProgressBar.tsx` - Step progress indicator
7. `src/components/OnboardingModal/ObjectiveCard.tsx` - Selectable objective card
8. `src/lib/onboarding.ts` - Onboarding state persistence helpers
9. `src/lib/analytics.ts` - Event instrumentation helpers (optional)

**Modified files (3):**
1. `src/app/stories/page.tsx` - Trigger onboarding on first story creation
2. `src/lib/stories.ts` - Add `createStoryWithChapter` helper for onboarding flow
3. `src/lib/scenes.ts` - Ensure `createScene` supports scaffold text parameter

## Step-by-step plan

### Phase 1: Onboarding state management

**Step 1.1: Add onboarding state helpers**
- File: `src/lib/onboarding.ts` (new)
- `hasSeenOnboarding(): boolean` - Check if user has completed or skipped onboarding (localStorage key: `onboarding-completed`)
- `markOnboardingComplete(): void` - Set completion flag
- `markOnboardingSkipped(): void` - Set skipped flag (same localStorage key, value: `skipped`)
- `trackOnboardingStep(step: number, action: 'viewed' | 'continued' | 'skipped'): void` - Log step events (console.log for v1, placeholder for analytics)

**Step 1.2: Add story creation helper**
- File: `src/lib/stories.ts` (modify)
- Add `createStoryWithChapter(title: string, genre: string, chapterTitle: string, sceneTitle: string, scenePlaceholder: string): { storyId: string, sceneId: string }` 
- Creates story record, creates first chapter/scene with placeholder content, returns IDs for routing
- Uses existing story/scene localStorage patterns

### Phase 2: Core modal and step components

**Step 2.1: Build ProgressBar component**
- Files: `src/components/OnboardingModal/ProgressBar.tsx`, inline CSS or parent module
- Props: `currentStep: number`, `totalSteps: number`
- Render: "Step X of Y" text + segmented progress bar (3 segments, filled up to current step)
- Accessibility: `aria-label="Onboarding progress"`, current step announced via `aria-live="polite"`
- Style: Compact, grayscale tokens, horizontal bar with filled/unfilled segments

**Step 2.2: Build StoryNameStep component**
- File: `src/components/OnboardingModal/StoryNameStep.tsx`
- Props: `storyName: string`, `genre: string`, `onStoryNameChange: (name: string) => void`, `onGenreChange: (genre: string) => void`
- Render: 
  - Input for story name (placeholder: "Untitled Story", autofocus on mount)
  - Genre select or chip group (options: Fiction, Non-Fiction, Fantasy, Sci-Fi, Mystery, Romance, Other)
- Validation: Story name optional (defaults to "Untitled Story"), genre optional
- Style: Vertical layout, generous spacing, grayscale tokens

**Step 2.3: Build ChapterSceneStep component**
- File: `src/components/OnboardingModal/ChapterSceneStep.tsx`
- Props: `chapterTitle: string`, `sceneTitle: string`, `onChapterTitleChange`, `onSceneTitleChange`
- Render:
  - Input for chapter title (placeholder: "Chapter 1")
  - Input for scene title (placeholder: "Opening Scene")
  - Small preview card showing scaffold text (read-only, preview what will appear in editor)
- Validation: Both fields optional (defaults: "Chapter 1", "Opening Scene")
- Style: Vertical layout, preview card with subtle border, muted text

**Step 2.4: Build ObjectiveCard component**
- File: `src/components/OnboardingModal/ObjectiveCard.tsx`
- Props: `title: string`, `description: string`, `selected: boolean`, `onClick: () => void`
- Render: Card with title, 1-2 line description, selected state (border highlight)
- Accessibility: `role="radio"`, keyboard-selectable (Space/Enter), focus visible
- Style: Horizontal layout (desktop) or stacked (mobile), selected card has darker border

**Step 2.5: Build ObjectiveStep component**
- File: `src/components/OnboardingModal/ObjectiveStep.tsx`
- Props: `objective: string | null`, `onObjectiveChange: (obj: string) => void`
- Render: Three ObjectiveCard components (Draft, Revise, Brainstorm) with descriptions:
  - Draft: "Start writing your first draft"
  - Revise: "Edit and improve existing text"
  - Brainstorm: "Explore ideas and outlines"
- Single-select behavior (radio group pattern)
- Validation: Optional (defaults to "Draft" if not selected)
- Style: Horizontal card grid on desktop, vertical stack on mobile

### Phase 3: Main modal container

**Step 3.1: Build OnboardingModal component**
- Files: `src/components/OnboardingModal/OnboardingModal.tsx`, `.module.css`
- Props: `open: boolean`, `onClose: () => void`, `onComplete: (data: OnboardingData) => void`
- State: `currentStep: number`, form data for all three steps
- Render:
  - Modal overlay (fixed position, backdrop)
  - Centered panel (max-width, single column)
  - ProgressBar at top
  - Current step component (StoryNameStep, ChapterSceneStep, or ObjectiveStep)
  - Footer with Back, Continue/Create, Skip buttons
- Navigation:
  - "Continue" advances to next step (validates optional fields, shows defaults)
  - "Back" returns to previous step
  - "Skip onboarding" closes modal, marks skipped
  - Step 3 "Create scene" button calls `onComplete` with form data
- Keyboard: Tab order through inputs and buttons, Escape closes modal (shows skip confirmation)
- Accessibility: `role="dialog"`, `aria-labelledby` for heading, focus trap, restore focus on close
- Style: White background, subtle shadow, grayscale borders, generous padding

**Step 3.2: Wire up step transitions**
- Track form data across steps (state in OnboardingModal)
- Validate each step before advancing (allow empty fields with defaults)
- Track step view/continue/skip events via `trackOnboardingStep`
- On "Create scene": call `onComplete` with all form data, parent handles story creation + routing

### Phase 4: Integration with Stories page

**Step 4.1: Add onboarding trigger to Stories page**
- File: `src/app/stories/page.tsx` (modify)
- Add state: `const [showOnboarding, setShowOnboarding] = useState(false)`
- On mount: If `!hasSeenOnboarding()` and user clicks "Create story", show onboarding modal instead of default flow
- Handle onboarding completion:
  - Call `createStoryWithChapter(data)` to create story/chapter/scene
  - Call `markOnboardingComplete()`
  - Route to `/story/[storyId]` with editor mode
- Handle skip: Call `markOnboardingSkipped()`, allow normal story creation

**Step 4.2: Ensure editor loads with scaffold**
- File: `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` (verify existing behavior)
- When routed from onboarding, scene content should contain placeholder text
- Verify cursor focuses in editor textarea on mount
- Breadcrumb shows chapter/scene titles from onboarding

### Phase 5: Loading states and error handling

**Step 5.1: Add loading state for story creation**
- In OnboardingModal: Add `isCreating: boolean` state
- On "Create scene" click: Set `isCreating = true`, show inline spinner with text "Creating your first scene..."
- Disable all form controls and navigation buttons while creating
- On success: Show brief "Your first scene is ready" message (<1s), then route to editor
- On error: Show inline error banner at top of modal, preserve form data, allow retry

**Step 5.2: Handle empty state for users who skip**
- File: `src/app/stories/page.tsx`
- If user skips onboarding and has zero stories, show enhanced empty state:
  - Message: "Ready to start writing? Create your first story."
  - CTA: "Create story" button (bypasses onboarding on subsequent clicks)
- Reassurance text: "You can create chapters and scenes as you write."

### Phase 6: Responsive behavior and polish

**Step 6.1: Mobile responsive layout**
- File: `src/components/OnboardingModal/OnboardingModal.module.css`
- Desktop: Centered modal, max-width ~500px, vertical padding
- Mobile (<768px): Full-height bottom sheet, sticky footer with actions
- All steps: Single-column layout on mobile, no side-by-side inputs
- Objective cards: Stack vertically on mobile instead of horizontal grid

**Step 6.2: Polish and microcopy**
- Step 1 heading: "Start your first story"
- Step 2 heading: "Set up your first scene"
- Step 3 heading: "Choose your writing goal"
- Success message: "Your first scene is ready"
- Skip button text: "Skip onboarding" (not "Skip" alone for clarity)
- Back button: Hidden on step 1, visible on steps 2-3

**Step 6.3: Focus management**
- On modal open: Focus first input (story name)
- On step advance: Focus first input of next step
- On modal close: Restore focus to trigger button on Stories page

## Test plan

**Unit tests (if framework exists):**
- `src/lib/onboarding.ts`: Test `hasSeenOnboarding`, `markOnboardingComplete`, persistence
- Story creation: Test `createStoryWithChapter` creates story + chapter + scene with placeholder

**Integration tests:**
1. First-time user clicks "Create story" → Onboarding modal opens
2. Complete all 3 steps → Story/chapter/scene created → Routes to editor with scaffold
3. Skip onboarding on step 1 → Modal closes, flag set, onboarding not shown again
4. User with existing stories → Onboarding not shown
5. Refresh page after skip → Onboarding stays hidden
6. Complete onboarding, create second story → Onboarding not shown

**Manual testing:**

- **Keyboard accessibility:**
  - Tab through all inputs and buttons in logical order
  - Enter/Space activates buttons and selects objective cards
  - Escape closes modal (with confirmation if in progress)
  - Focus visible on all interactive elements
  - Screen reader announces step progress

- **Responsive:**
  - Desktop: Centered modal, horizontal objective cards
  - Mobile: Full-height sheet, sticky footer, stacked objective cards
  - Modal does not break layout on small screens

- **User flows:**
  - Complete onboarding with all fields filled → Lands in editor with correct titles
  - Complete onboarding with empty fields → Defaults applied (Untitled Story, Chapter 1, Opening Scene)
  - Skip on step 1 → No story created, can create manually
  - Skip on step 2 → Modal closes, onboarding marked skipped
  - Back button navigates to previous step without losing data

- **Edge cases:**
  - Very long story/chapter/scene names (50+ chars): Truncate in preview, full value saved
  - Network error during creation: Error banner shown, retry available
  - User closes tab mid-onboarding: Onboarding state not marked complete, shows again on next visit
  - User has onboarding-completed flag but zero stories: Can still create stories normally

**Commands:**
```bash
npm run dev
# Visit http://localhost:3000/stories
# Clear localStorage key: onboarding-completed
# Click "Create story" → Should show onboarding
# Complete flow, verify lands in editor
# Return to /stories, try creating second story → Onboarding should not appear
```

## Risks and mitigations

**Risk 1: Modal blocks experienced users**
- Problem: Power users forced to skip every time if flag fails
- Impact: Frustration, friction in story creation flow
- Mitigation: Make skip button prominent, one-click action. Persist flag reliably in localStorage. Future: Add user preference toggle in settings.

**Risk 2: Placeholder text feels prescriptive**
- Problem: Users feel locked into suggested structure
- Impact: Discourages experimentation
- Mitigation: Use minimal, neutral placeholder ("Start writing..."). Clearly communicate that titles/text are editable. Future: Offer blank scaffold option.

**Risk 3: Onboarding feels slow compared to direct creation**
- Problem: 3 steps take longer than one-click "New story"
- Impact: Activation improvement offset by time-to-write
- Mitigation: Allow empty fields with smart defaults. Keep each step single-action. Track time-to-completion in analytics to validate <2min target.

**Risk 4: Onboarding state tied to localStorage is fragile**
- Problem: Clearing browser data resets onboarding flag, users see it again
- Impact: Annoyance, perceived bug
- Mitigation: Accept localStorage limitation for v1 (no backend yet). Future: Sync onboarding state to user profile when auth is added.

**Risk 5: Objective selection has no functional impact in v1**
- Problem: Selecting "Draft" vs "Revise" doesn't change editor behavior
- Impact: Feels like false choice, reduces trust
- Mitigation: Keep step 3 lightweight. Future: Use objective to customize editor onboarding tooltips or suggested actions.

## Rollback strategy

**If critical bugs:**
1. Revert PR merge commit
2. Story creation flow returns to pre-onboarding behavior (direct creation)
3. No data loss: Stories/scenes created via onboarding persist normally

**Feature flag (future):**
- Add `ENABLE_ONBOARDING` environment variable
- If false, bypass onboarding modal entirely
- Allows instant rollback without code deploy

**Graceful degradation:**
- If onboarding state helpers fail: Show onboarding every time (annoying but safe)
- If story creation fails: Show error, allow retry or manual creation
- If modal rendering crashes: Wrap in error boundary, fall back to direct story creation

## Observability

**Logs:**
- Console log each onboarding event: "Onboarding: step 1 viewed", "Onboarding: step 2 continued", "Onboarding: completed", "Onboarding: skipped"
- Log story creation: "Created story via onboarding: {storyId}"

**Metrics (future analytics):**
- Track onboarding funnel: Step 1 viewed → Step 2 viewed → Step 3 viewed → Completed
- Track skip rate by step: "Skipped on step X"
- Track time-to-completion (target: <2 minutes)
- Track activation rate: % of users who complete onboarding and write 200+ words in first session

**Event instrumentation points:**
- `onboarding_step_viewed` (step number)
- `onboarding_step_continued` (step number)
- `onboarding_skipped` (step number)
- `onboarding_completed` (time elapsed)
- `first_scene_created` (via onboarding vs. manual)
- `editor_first_200_words` (milestone for activation)

## Acceptance mapping

| Acceptance Criteria | Implementation |
|---------------------|----------------|
| New user is offered onboarding once after first story creation | Stories page checks `hasSeenOnboarding()`, shows modal if false, marks complete/skip on finish |
| User can complete onboarding in under 2 minutes and land in editable first scene | 3 quick steps with defaults → `createStoryWithChapter` → route to editor with focus on textarea |
| User can skip onboarding at any step and still access core editor | "Skip onboarding" button in footer → `markOnboardingSkipped()` → modal closes, normal story creation available |
| Onboarding completion/skip state persists and prevents repeated prompting | localStorage key `onboarding-completed` checked on mount, never shown again after complete/skip |
| Event instrumentation exists for each onboarding step and completion outcome | `trackOnboardingStep` logs each step view/continue/skip action, completion tracked on final step |

## Handoff

**Implementation readiness:**
- ✅ Product requirements reviewed
- ✅ Design brief reviewed (3-step modal, skip controls, story creation flow)
- ✅ Current Stories page and Editor route architecture understood
- ✅ Implementation plan complete with 6 phases

**Code Monkey: Key implementation notes**
1. **Phase order:** State management → Components → Modal → Integration → Loading/errors → Polish
2. **Skip controls:** Must be prominent and one-click (not buried in nested dialogs)
3. **Defaults:** All form fields optional, apply sensible defaults (Untitled Story, Chapter 1, Opening Scene)
4. **localStorage:** Use key `onboarding-completed` with values `"completed"` or `"skipped"`
5. **Focus management:** Autofocus first input on each step, restore focus on modal close
6. **Event instrumentation:** Console.log for v1, structured for future analytics integration
7. **Refer to Design brief** for exact microcopy, step descriptions, and visual hierarchy

title: Add Story Compass panel to keep premise, stakes, and promises visible while drafting
description:
## Problem
Long-form drafts drift when a writer cannot quickly see the story's core promise (premise, stakes, and key setup/payoff commitments) during scene-level writing and revision. That drift creates expensive late-stage rewrites.

## Target user
Novel writers drafting across many sessions who need lightweight narrative alignment without leaving the editor.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase % of active stories with explicit story premise + stakes captured
- Reduce late-stage structural rewrites caused by premise drift
- Increase weekly usage of in-editor alignment tools

## Scope
- Add a collapsible `Story Compass` panel in the story editor showing story-level fields: premise, central conflict, stakes, and key promises to pay off
- Allow writers to pin 3-8 active promises (setup items) and mark payoff status (`open`, `in progress`, `resolved`)
- Surface a per-scene quick check: `This scene advances` (premise / stakes / promise) with multi-select tags
- Add filters in scene navigation to find scenes not mapped to premise/stakes/promises
- Persist data at story and scene levels so it is available across drafting and revision workflows

## Non-goals
- Automated AI-generated premise diagnostics
- Full plotting methodology frameworks (Save the Cat, Hero's Journey templates, etc.)
- Public-facing reader analytics

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can create/edit premise, conflict, stakes, and promise entries from the Story Compass
- Writer can assign one or more Story Compass tags to any scene
- Navigator can filter to scenes with no Story Compass alignment tags
- Promise status changes persist and are visible in the Story Compass list
- Story Compass can be collapsed/expanded without disrupting editor focus

## Priority rationale
This is a writer-flow differentiator, not parity: competitors are strong on structure surfaces, but fewer tools keep the story promise continuously visible inside drafting. Shipping this now improves manuscript coherence and aligns directly with the goal to ship a credible, writer-first story app.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

This feature stands out when positioned as **continuous narrative alignment inside drafting**, not just planning metadata. Competitors offer planning boards/tags, but keeping premise, stakes, and setup/payoff promises visible and actionable at scene time reduces drift in the writing moment.

## Competitor evidence
- LivingWriter emphasizes planning structures and element management (planning grid for plot points/scenes and element tags/relationships), but this is primarily organization-first rather than a dedicated in-draft promise tracker: https://guides.livingwriter.com/product-documentation/boards/planning-board/standard-grid and https://guides.livingwriter.com/product-documentation/chapters-and-elements/elements-section
- Novelcrafter supports labels and matrix/codex tracking across scenes, and explicitly notes there is no dedicated place for a book summary (as of Nov 2024), indicating a gap in always-on story-level guidance while drafting: https://docs.novelcrafter.com/en/articles/8677718-labeling-your-scenes , https://docs.novelcrafter.com/en/articles/8675733-the-plan-interface , https://docs.novelcrafter.com/en/articles/9419387-where-do-i-put-my-scene-chapter-act-book-summary-what-about-my-beats
- Campfire highlights modular tools and note sidebars in manuscript workflows, but messaging is broad module flexibility rather than explicit promise-to-scene accountability: https://www.campfirewriting.com/ and https://www.campfirewriting.com/pricing

## Risks / trade-offs
- Extra scene-tagging friction can hurt drafting flow if interaction cost is too high.
- Promise status semantics (`open/in progress/resolved`) can become inconsistent without clear UX constraints.
- If over-specified, this can feel like prescriptive plotting instead of writer support.

## Recommendation
Proceed, but anchor MVP on fast in-editor interactions: one-click scene alignment tags, frictionless collapse/expand, and low-cognitive-load promise tracking. Success should be measured by reduced unaligned scenes and fewer late revision loops, not just panel usage.

Assign to: Design

title: Build Beta Feedback Digest that clusters reader comments into actionable chapter insights
description:
## Problem
Raw beta-reader comments are valuable but noisy. Writers struggle to convert scattered feedback into prioritized revision decisions, so useful insight gets lost and revision cycles slow down.

## Target user
Writers using private chapter sharing who receive reader comments and need fast synthesis before revision passes.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase % of beta-feedback comments that result in completed revision actions
- Reduce time from feedback receipt to first revision action
- Increase repeat usage of chapter sharing + feedback workflow

## Scope
- Add a `Feedback Digest` view that groups incoming reader comments by chapter and theme (clarity, pacing, character, confusion, emotional impact)
- Provide confidence indicators based on repeated signals (e.g., 5 readers flagged pacing in Chapter 4)
- Allow writer to convert any digest cluster into a revision task in the Revision Queue or scene note
- Include reader-trust controls: exclude outlier comments and hide anonymous low-signal inputs
- Support export of digest summary for external editing review

## Non-goals
- Public social feed or story discovery marketplace
- Automatic acceptance/rejection of feedback without writer choice
- Replacing raw comment view entirely

## Acceptance criteria
- Digest groups feedback by chapter and at least five predefined theme buckets
- Writer can open a digest cluster and inspect underlying source comments
- Writer can convert a digest item into a revision task in one flow
- Writer can exclude a comment from digest weighting and see aggregate values recalculate
- Digest updates when new chapter comments arrive

## Priority rationale
Inkitt-like feedback loops are useful, but differentiation comes from turning feedback into decisive revision actions inside the writing workspace. This feature creates that bridge and strengthens our writer-first revision workflow.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Add distraction-free Focus Mode for deep drafting sessions
description:
## Problem
Writers lose momentum when the editor UI competes for attention, especially during long drafting sessions. The current product has strong planning surfaces but lacks an explicit distraction-free drafting mode similar to the best writer-first tools.

## Target user
Daily fiction and long-form writers who do focused drafting sprints and want a clean, low-noise writing environment.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase average words written per active writing session
- Increase percentage of sessions longer than 20 minutes
- Increase weekly active writers who complete at least 3 focused sessions

## Scope
- Add a toggleable Focus Mode in the editor that minimizes non-essential UI (left/right side panels, secondary controls, visual noise).
- Keep only core drafting controls visible: title, body editor, exit focus mode, and minimal save/status indicator.
- Add typewriter-scroll option (cursor line stays in upper-middle viewport) with persisted preference.
- Add optional session timer presets (15/30/45 minutes) with lightweight end-of-session summary (time and words written).
- Persist focus preferences per user/browser.

## Non-goals
- Gamification systems (badges, streak rewards, social leaderboards)
- AI writing assistance changes in this ticket
- Redesigning the full editor outside Focus Mode

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can enter and exit Focus Mode from the story editor with one action.
- In Focus Mode, non-essential panels are hidden and the editor remains fully usable with keyboard shortcuts.
- Typewriter-scroll can be toggled on/off and persists across refresh.
- Session timer can start from preset durations and shows elapsed/remaining time without blocking writing.
- Exiting a completed timer session shows a summary with words written in that session.

## Priority rationale
This is a core differentiator for writer trust and daily habit formation. Living Writer’s clarity is a key inspiration; shipping a superior focused drafting experience now can improve retention before expanding more advanced tooling.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

## UX goal
Create a one-action Focus Mode that removes chrome and keeps writers in uninterrupted drafting flow, while preserving confidence signals (save state, timer state, easy exit).

## Information hierarchy
1. Primary: story title + body editor (always visible in Focus Mode).
2. Secondary: minimal focus utility rail (exit focus, typewriter toggle, timer status/control).
3. Tertiary: save/status indicator (visible but low prominence).
4. Hidden in Focus Mode: global nav, app header, right notes sidebar, view mode switch, other non-drafting controls.

## Layout and components
- In normal editor view, retain current two-column editor + notes layout.
- Entering Focus Mode collapses to a single centered drafting canvas with generous horizontal margins and reduced visual boundaries.
- Keep title input at top of canvas, then body editor occupying remaining height.
- Add a compact top-right utility cluster inside the story editor canvas (not global app header):
  - Exit Focus button
  - Typewriter scroll toggle
  - Timer control (preset picker + running state)
  - Save/status text
- Timer completion summary should appear as a lightweight inline sheet anchored near the utility cluster (non-blocking; dismissible; keyboard focusable).

## Interaction states
- Idle: clean canvas, muted utility controls until hover/focus, active writing cursor in editor.
- Loading: preserve editor skeleton shape and keep title/editor placeholders in final positions to avoid layout shift.
- Empty: editor placeholder copy remains instructional and calm; timer controls still available.
- Error: show compact inline error near utility cluster (e.g., timer persistence failure) without covering editor text.
- Success: timer-complete summary shows session duration + words written, then collapses back to minimal status after dismissal/timeout.

## Accessibility
- Keyboard: one-action enter/exit must be reachable via keyboard; utility controls tab in logical order before editor body.
- Focus: strong visible focus ring using existing dark grayscale token, including on compact icon/button controls.
- Contrast: keep text and controls at AA contrast against white/polar backgrounds; avoid low-contrast timer text.
- Text clarity: avoid jargon in timer labels; use explicit labels ("15 min sprint", "Typewriter scroll", "Exit Focus").

## Visual style
- Stay within approved grayscale tokens from `src/styles/tokens.css`.
- Base surfaces: `--color-white` / `--color-polar-100`; separators with `--color-fashion-300`.
- Primary text: `--color-black-700`; secondary utility text: `--color-elephant-500` or `--color-cement-400`.
- Preserve editorial typography feel already used in textarea (serif body), with minimal ornament and low-noise controls.

## Responsive behavior
- Desktop: centered writing column with max readable line length; utility controls in top-right cluster.
- Tablet: keep single-column focus canvas; timer/typewriter controls remain visible, compact spacing.
- Mobile: Focus Mode still one action; keep only essential controls in a single-row compact bar above editor, no floating overlapping panels.

## Codebase alignment
Reviewed and aligned with current architecture:
- `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`
- `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css`
- `src/components/AppShell/AppShell.tsx`
- `src/components/ContentContainer/ContentContainer.tsx`
- `src/components/ViewModeSwitch/ViewModeSwitch.tsx`
- `src/styles/tokens.css`

## Reuse vs new build
- Reuse: existing story editor route/layout, title input, textarea editor, token palette, focus-ring style patterns, and existing button/toolbar interaction conventions from `ViewModeSwitch`.
- New build: a focused utility control group (exit/typewriter/timer), timer preset UI, and end-of-session summary component specific to Focus Mode.
- Avoid creating a second editor screen; use mode-based UI state in the existing story editor page.

- Typewriter scroll is stateful behavior on the textarea scroll position; ensure it gracefully degrades when content is short.
- Timer summary requires capturing word count delta at session start/end; define source of truth consistently with current editor content state.
- Preference persistence should be browser/user scoped and non-blocking if storage is unavailable.

- Anchor Focus Mode state in `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` and style variants in `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css` (e.g., `data-focus-mode` state classing).

- `src/components/FocusControls/FocusControls.tsx` + `.module.css` — compact utility cluster in focus canvas

**Modified files:**
- `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` — add focus mode state, Focus button in header, FocusControls in canvas, data-focus-mode on root
- `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css` — focus mode layout overrides, typewriter scroll, FocusControls positioning

## Step-by-step plan

1. **Create `useFocusMode` hook** (`src/hooks/useFocusMode.ts`):
   - State: `isFocusMode: boolean`, `typewriterScroll: boolean`
   - Load `typewriterScroll` pref from `localStorage.getItem('focus_prefs')` on mount; parse JSON safely
   - `enterFocus()` → set `isFocusMode = true`
   - `exitFocus()` → set `isFocusMode = false`
   - `toggleTypewriter()` → toggle `typewriterScroll`, persist to `localStorage.setItem('focus_prefs', JSON.stringify({ typewriterScroll }))`
   - `useEffect`: add `keydown` listener for `Escape` to call `exitFocus()` while `isFocusMode`; clean up on unmount or mode exit
   - Export: `{ isFocusMode, typewriterScroll, enterFocus, exitFocus, toggleTypewriter }`

2. **Create `useSessionTimer` hook** (`src/hooks/useSessionTimer.ts`):
   - State: `timerState: 'idle' | 'running' | 'complete'`, `elapsedMs: number`, `targetMs: number | null`, `startWordCount: number`
   - `startTimer(durationMs: number, currentWordCount: number)` → sets target, records startWordCount, sets state to `'running'`
   - `stopTimer()` → clears interval, returns to `'idle'`
   - `dismissSummary()` → resets to `'idle'`
   - While `'running'`: `setInterval(1000)` increments `elapsedMs`; when `elapsedMs >= targetMs`, set state to `'complete'`, clear interval
   - Word count: pass current content to hook, compute `wordsWritten = currentWordCount - startWordCount`
   - Export: `{ timerState, elapsedMs, targetMs, startTimer, stopTimer, dismissSummary }`
   - Helper: `formatDuration(ms: number): string` → `"14:32"` or `"45:00"`

3. **Create `FocusControls` component** (`src/components/FocusControls/FocusControls.tsx`):
   - Props: `typewriterScroll`, `onToggleTypewriter`, `timerState`, `elapsedMs`, `targetMs`, `onStartTimer(durationMs)`, `onStopTimer`, `onDismissSummary`, `wordsWritten`, `onExitFocus`, `saveState`, `lastSaved`
   - Layout: compact top-right cluster inside the editor canvas (position absolute, top-right corner)
   - Controls:
     - "Exit focus" button (label: "Exit focus")
     - Typewriter scroll toggle (label: "Typewriter scroll", `aria-pressed`)
     - Timer section:
       - Idle: preset picker buttons (15 min | 30 min | 45 min), clicking one calls `onStartTimer`
       - Running: elapsed/remaining display + "Stop" button
       - Complete: inline summary card — "Session complete · {duration} · {wordsWritten} words written" + Dismiss button
     - Save status: muted text (reuse SaveStatus styling pattern from `src/components/SaveStatus`)
   - All interactive elements: `type="button"`, `aria-label`, keyboard accessible via natural tab order
   - Styles: `position: absolute; top: var(--spacing-md); right: var(--spacing-md); z-index: var(--z-sticky)`; `--color-white` background, `--color-fashion-300` border, `--border-radius-md`

4. **Update `page.tsx`**:
   - Import and call `useFocusMode()` → `{ isFocusMode, typewriterScroll, enterFocus, exitFocus, toggleTypewriter }`
   - Import and call `useSessionTimer()` → timer state
   - Compute `wordCount = content.trim().split(/\s+/).filter(Boolean).length`
   - Add `data-focus-mode={isFocusMode ? 'true' : undefined}` to the outermost `.page` div
   - In editor mode `.editorHeader`: add a "Focus" button that calls `enterFocus()`; hide this button when `isFocusMode` (FocusControls handles exit)
   - In editor mode `.editorCanvas`: when `isFocusMode`, render `<FocusControls>` with all required props
   - Pass `data-typewriter-scroll={typewriterScroll ? 'true' : undefined}` to the `<textarea>`

5. **Update `page.module.css`**:
   - Focus mode layout overrides via `.page[data-focus-mode='true']`:
     - `.editorLayout`: `grid-template-columns: 1fr` (hide sidebar column)
     - `.editorMain`: `max-width: 720px; margin: 0 auto; width: 100%`
     - `.editorHeader` → hide ViewModeSwitch and SaveStatus (`.page[data-focus-mode='true'] .editorHeader > *:not(.titleInput)` `display: none`)
     - `.sidebar`: `display: none`
   - Typewriter scroll: `.editor[data-typewriter-scroll='true']` — handled via JS in `useFocusMode` effect using `textarea.scrollTop` adjustment on `input` event (set `scroll-padding-top` and adjust scroll to keep cursor line at ~30% viewport height)
   - `.focusControlsWrap` — absolute positioning inside `.editorCanvas` for the FocusControls cluster; hide when not in focus mode
   - Responsive:
     - Desktop: centered column `max-width: 720px`
     - Tablet: full-width with generous padding
     - Mobile: `FocusControls` becomes a single compact row at top of canvas

6. **Typewriter scroll implementation** (in `useFocusMode.ts`):
   - Add a `useEffect` that attaches `input` event listener to the textarea ref (pass ref as optional param)
   - On `input`: calculate `caretOffset = textarea.selectionStart`, measure line position relative to viewport height, adjust `textarea.scrollTop` so cursor stays at ~30% from top
   - Graceful degrade: if content < viewport height, do not force scroll

## Test plan

- **Enter focus**: click "Focus" button → sidebar hides, ViewModeSwitch hides, AppShell nav visible check (only page-level chrome is hidden in v1), centered canvas appears
- **Exit focus**: "Exit focus" button in FocusControls → all UI restored; Escape key also exits
- **Typewriter scroll**: type content past viewport → cursor stays near top-third of screen; toggle off → normal scroll resumes; preference persists across page reload
- **Timer start**: click "30 min" → timer runs, shows elapsed/remaining live; title area still fully editable during timer
- **Timer complete**: wait for timer to reach 0 (or use short test duration) → summary card shows duration + words written; click Dismiss → returns to idle
- **Word count delta**: note starting word count, write 50 words, complete timer → summary shows approximately 50
- **Persistence**: reload while typewriter pref on → pref loads from localStorage
- **Keyboard**: Tab to Focus button → Enter enters mode; Tab through FocusControls → all controls reachable; Enter/Space activates each
- **No modal blocking**: timer summary is inline, never blocks typing
- Manual check: story editor textarea remains fully usable throughout all modes

## Risks and mitigations

- **AppShell global nav hiding**: in v1, only page-level chrome (sidebar, ViewModeSwitch, header controls) is hidden via CSS on `.page` root; AppShell header remains visible to avoid complex inter-component communication. If user feedback requires full chrome hide, a follow-up ticket can add a global CSS class via `document.body`
- **Typewriter scroll browser inconsistency**: test on Chrome/Safari/Firefox; add `scrollIntoView` fallback if `scrollTop` calculation is unreliable
- **Word count simplification**: whitespace-split word count is intentionally simple for v1; matches common writer tool conventions

## Rollback strategy
All code is additive. Removing the "Focus" button from `.editorHeader` in `page.tsx` fully hides the feature. The `data-focus-mode` attribute has no effect when never set to `'true'`.

## Observability
- `console.log('FocusMode: entered/exited')` on mode transitions
- `console.log('SessionTimer: completed', { duration, wordsWritten })` on timer completion

## Acceptance mapping
- "Writer can enter and exit Focus Mode from the story editor with one action" → Focus button + Escape key
- "Non-essential panels are hidden and editor remains fully usable" → CSS `data-focus-mode` hides sidebar, ViewModeSwitch, header controls
- "Typewriter-scroll can be toggled on/off and persists across refresh" → `useFocusMode` persists to localStorage
- "Session timer can start from preset durations and shows elapsed/remaining time" → `useSessionTimer` + `FocusControls` timer section
- "Exiting a completed timer session shows a summary with words written" → inline summary card in `FocusControls`

## Handoff

Assign to: Code Monkey


</plan>

title: Create submission package builder for query letter, synopses, and beta-reader packet
description:
## Problem
Writers can draft manuscripts but still face major friction when preparing submission deliverables. They must manually assemble query letters, synopses, and beta-reader materials in separate tools, causing inconsistency and duplicated effort.

## Target user
Writers approaching submission-readiness who need a repeatable handoff workflow from manuscript to external deliverables.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase percentage of completed manuscripts that generate at least one submission package
- Reduce time from draft-complete to first external submission/share event
- Improve perceived end-to-end workflow satisfaction

## Scope
- Add `Submission Package` workspace at story level
- Generate editable templates for: query letter, one-page synopsis, long synopsis, and beta-reader briefing sheet
- Auto-link core manuscript metadata (title, genre, logline, comps, protagonist/conflict placeholders) into templates
- Include versioning for package drafts with timestamps and status labels (draft, ready, submitted)
- Export package as DOCX bundle and plain-text copy blocks

## Non-goals
- Agent/discovery marketplace integrations
- Automated email sending or CRM workflow
- Legal contract generation

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can create a submission package from an existing story in under 5 minutes
- All package documents are editable and save independently
- Metadata changes can be refreshed into templates with explicit user confirmation
- Writer can export package artifacts without losing template structure
- Package status and version history persist per story

## Priority rationale
Market Research identified publishing handoff as a concrete gap where competitors are weaker. This feature expands the app from drafting tool to full manuscript workflow, improving adoption and retention near the finish line.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research
